content.full_text:
City of Berkeley

Office of the City Attorney
Martin Luther King, Jr.
Civic Center Building
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, California 94704
Telecommunications Device for the Deaf (415) 644-6915

Telephone: (415) 644-6380
Telecopy: (415) 644-8641

July 15, 1991


California Fair Political
Practices Commission
Attn: Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento CA 95804-0807

Re: Letter No. 91-121

Dear Mr. Rothman:

This is to follow-up on the advice provided in your June 6, 1991
letter. In your letter, it was advised that it would be
inappropriate for the Berkeley Rent Stabilization Board (Board)
to appropriate funds to pay the legal costs incurred by Board
Commissioners David Bryden and Kathleen DeVries in defending a
lawsuit challenging their election to the Board to the extent
that "the defense of such litigation resulted in some unique and
personal benefit to the candidates". However, it was also stated
that to the extent the facts indicate that such litigation is
for the purpose of explicating the City's interpretation of its
Charter," the appropriation of funds would be permissible. I am
requesting a determination of whether Commissioners Bryden and
DeVries received a unique and personal benefit from defense of
the litigation. The pertinent facts are as follows:

In 1982, the voters of Berkeley amended the City Charter to
create an elected Rent Board of nine commissioners. Under
Article XVII, Section 122(1) of the Charter, the term of office
for Board Commissioners is defined as follows:

Commissioners shall serve terms beginning on the first
day of the month following their election. Terms shall
be four years, except as provided by Subsection (4)
below. Commissioners shall serve a maximum of two full
terms. (Emphasis added.)

[wheelchair symbol] Telecommunications Device for the Deaf (415) 644-6915

Page 2
Letter to Jonathan Rothman
July 15, 1991


However, under Article XVII Section 122(4), the terms of office
of the first elected Board were stated to be:

     The nine commissioners elected under Subsection (3), of
     the five commissioners receiving the least votes shall
     hold office until the last day of November in the next
     even numbered year. The four remaining board
     commissioners shall hold office for an additional two
     years, those terms also ending on the last day of
     November of the appropriate year.

Commissioner Bryden and DeVries were elected in 1984 and were
among the five commissioners receiving the least number of votes.
Both commissioners were re-elected in 1986 and 1990. Following
the November 1990 election, a lawsuit was brought on the grounds
that they had already served two "full terms" on the Board, and
therefore had been ineligible to run for election in 1990. The
court ultimately found that the initial two year term was not a
"full term" and Mr. Bryden and Ms. DeVries had been properly
elected.

Clearly, the determination of whether the two year term served by
five commissioners of the first elected Rent Board constituted a
"full term" under Article XVII Section 122(1) of the Berkeley
City Charter involved an interpretation of the Charter.
Arguably, if the analysis were to end here, it would appear that
it would be appropriate for the Board to pay the litigation costs
incurred in defending the election challenge. However, it must
be noted that the issue of the two year term will never arise
again and the only Commissioners affected by the outcome of the
litigation were Mr. Bryden and Ms. DeVries. Additionally, the
Board intervened in the action on its own behalf. The question,
therefore, is whether these aspects of the litigation result in
Commissioners Bryden and DeVries being provided a unique and
personal benefit which precludes the Board from now paying their
litigation expenses.

A second issue of concern is whether the manner in which the
litigation expenses have been paid now precludes the Board from
appropriating money to pay the costs or from reimbursing the
political action committees which actually paid them. After the
election challenge was filed, an item was put on the Board's
agenda to pay the litigation costs of defending his action.
However, after receiving oral advice from your office that this
did not appear to be an appropriate use of public funds, no
action was taken on the item. Commissioners Bryden and DeVries

Page 3
Letter to Jonathan Rothman
July 15, 1991


jointly obtained private counsel and incurred over $15,000 in
legal costs defending the suit; these these expenses have been
paid in part by the Bryden Committee and the DeVries Committee -
political action committees controlled by the respective
commissioners. Does the fact that the expenses have already been
paid by these committees preclude the Board from paying the
actual legal costs to the attorney who represented the
commissioners? Understandably, the money paid by the committees
would be returned to the committees.

Finally, assuming that payment of the legal costs is otherwise
appropriate, would it be a conflict of interest for Commissioners
Bryden and DeVries to participate in the discussion and vote on a
motion to pay the costs?

Thank you very much for your attention to this matter. Please
call me if you have any questions.

Sincerely,

BRIAN KELLY
Deputy City Attorney

cc: David Bryden
    Kathleen DeVries

[illegible] cl
